Title: Sunway Construction wins RM1.75bil hyperscale data centre contract
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Sunway Construction Group Bhd has secured a RM1.75 billion contract to build a hyperscale data centre in Bandar Serendah, Selangor, the company said in a bourse filing.
The contract, awarded by an international hyperscaler to its unit Sunway Construction Sdn Bhd, covers the construction, completion, testing and commissioning of the facility.
The project will be undertaken on a build-only basis, encompassing core and shell works, campus infrastructure, as well as mechanical, electrical and plumbing fit-out.
Construction is slated to begin in the third quarter of 2026, with completion targeted by the third quarter of 2028.
Sunway Construction said the project is subject to typical construction risks, particularly fluctuations in material prices, but noted that its experience in large-scale projects should help mitigate these challenges.
The group does not expect a material impact on its earnings for the financial year ending Dec 31, 2026, but said the project is expected to contribute positively over the longer term.
Its outstanding order book stands at RM8.7 billion, including RM3 billion in new projects secured this year.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: AmBank steps up FDI push into Johor-Singapore SEZ, targets Chinese investors
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: AmBank Group is intensifying its efforts to drive foreign direct investment (FDI) into Malaysia, focusing on the high-interest Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone (JS-SEZ).
In a recent strategic move, the bank collaborated with the Johor state government to host an investment roundtable in Shanghai, targeting Chinese corporate leaders interested in the region's growing economic potential.
Leading the Johor delegation were Johor Menteri Besar Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi, State Investment Committee chairman Lee Ting Han, Invest Johor chief executive Natazha Hariss and Malaysia's consul general in Shanghai, Mohd Shahafeez Shahariz.
The roundtable drew about 30 Chinese corporate delegates, showcasing Johor as a premier regional hub for expansion.
Lee said that Johor's outreach efforts in Shanghai reflect the increasing importance of Chinese investors in the state's growth story.
"Financial institutions, particularly Malaysian-based institutions such as AmBank, are key partners in this journey. Their ability to provide tailored financing solutions and on-the-ground support gives investors the confidence to establish and expand operations in Johor," he added.
Since the JS-SEZ was formalised in January 2025, Johor has secured a historic RM110 billion in approved investments, the highest in Malaysian history, with significant contributions from China.
AmBank has played a direct role in facilitating this growth by approving roughly RM2 billion in financing for new FDI projects, reflecting its active involvement in supporting investment inflows that contribute to Johor's economic growth.
Managing director of business banking, Christopher Yap, said AmBank remains committed to providing comprehensive, end-to-end financial solutions tailored to the needs of international investors.
"The JS-SEZ represents a transformative opportunity for regional growth, and we are proud to support this initiative by connecting global investors with the right financial infrastructure, insights and partnerships to succeed," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Negeri Sembilan govt halts sand mining in Seremban river
Article:
SEREMBAN: The Negeri Sembilan government has instructed for sand mining activities in Sungai Kampung Jijan here to be stopped following complaints from residents over the activity.
State Entrepreneurship, Human Resources, Climate Change, Cooperatives and Consumerism Action Committee chairman S. Veerapan said the state only allows sand mining that adheres to standard operating procedures (SOP).
He warned that sand mining permits would be revoked immediately if SOPs are breached.
"Operators are responsible for restoring riverbanks damaged by their activities, with the costs borne entirely by the companies involved," he said when contacted today.
Veerapan said the prohibition is effective immediately, and operators will not be allowed to resume work until they comply with the regulations.
He added that sand mining is strictly prohibited in river areas located near water intakes.
At present, all river sand mining operations are granted to Yayasan Negeri Sembilan.
However, every operator must obtain permission from the state executive council and approvals from all relevant technical departments.
"Rivers are the primary water source for the people. Any future approvals will undergo strict and serious evaluation to ensure the safety and sustainability of water resources," he said.
Veerapan said that the permit for the company involved in the Kampung Jijan project had previously been suspended for a month.
"The Drainage and Irrigation Department (DID) and technical agencies found the company failed to address the allegations against it. We will cancel their permit entirely for breaching the conditions.
"The DID must monitor this as they provide the reciprocal approval. Following today's meeting, the company has been ordered to stop the project immediately as it has become a major issue," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MMHE partners South Korea's Hanwha Power on vessel projects
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia Marine and Heavy Engineering Holdings Bhd has teamed up with South Korea's Hanwha Power Systems Co Ltd to collaborate on selected newbuilding and vessel conversion projects.
In a filing with Bursa Malaysia, the company said its unit, Malaysia Marine and Heavy Engineering Sdn Bhd (MMHE), signed a memorandum of understanding with Hanwha Power on April 8, which also covers initiatives aligned with the net-zero agenda.
MMHE will serve as Hanwha Power's strategic shipyard partner in the region, providing construction and installation capabilities to deliver integrated, end-to-end solutions.
Hanwha Power, part of South Korea's Hanwha Group, will contribute engineering and procurement expertise to support selected newbuilding, as well as conversion and retrofit projects in areas of mutual strategic interest.
The agreement will remain valid for two years from the execution date, with an option for a one-year extension subject to mutual written agreement.
Malaysia Marine said the risks arising from the collaboration are expected to be minimal.
"In the event MMHE enters into a definitive agreement with Hanwha Power, the board and management will exercise due care in assessing the associated risks and benefits," it added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Selangor cops destroy ketum haul worth RM110,000
Article:
SHAH ALAM: Police in Selangor have destroyed more than half a tonne of ketum leaves and 2,929 litres of ketum liquid, with an estimated value of RM110,000.
State police chief Datuk Shazeli Kahar said the disposal was carried out on Tuesday by the Narcotic Crime Investigation Department (NCID).
"The disposal involved 278 investigation papers that had completed both investigation and prosecution processes in court," he said in a statement.
The cases were linked to several districts, including Shah Alam, Serdang, Sepang, Gombak and Hulu Selangor.
Shazeli said the destruction was conducted in accordance with strict standard operating procedures using the burial method.
The process was carried out in collaboration with the Shah Alam City Council's Public Cleansing and Waste Management Department and Worldwide Holdings at the Jeram sanitary landfill in Kuala Selangor.
He urged the public to continue cooperating with police by providing information on drug trafficking and abuse.
"Community support remains crucial in helping the NCID carry out its mission to combat drugs comprehensively," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia focusing on more resilient and diversified economy: Tengku Zafrul
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia is not only focusing on economic growth but also emphasising building a more resilient and diversified economy in the face of global uncertainties, said Tengku Datuk Seri Zafrul Abdul Aziz.
This follows the World Bank last week raising Malaysia's growth forecast to 4.4 per cent from 4.1 per cent for 2026, despite increasing global uncertainties, including the West Asia conflict.
Tengku Zafrul, who is the Malaysian Investment Development Authority (MIDA) chairman, said the development showed that the country's economy continued to grow but warned that Malaysia should not get too complacent.
"When global oil prices rise, the impact reaches the cost of goods here. When global trade is tense, our opportunities can also be affected.
"That is why our focus is not just on growth but also on building a more resilient and diversified economy," he said via his official Instagram.
He added that the World Bank's upward revision of the forecast was a positive sign, but the future of the country's economy depended on what was built at the moment.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Palm on track for second consecutive weekly loss as crude, soyoil weigh
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysian palm oil futures edged lower on Friday and were poised for a second straight weekly decline, weighed down by weaker crude oil prices and Chicago soyoil.
The benchmark palm oil contract for July delivery on the Bursa Malaysia Derivatives Exchange shed RM21 or 0.47 per cent, to RM4,474  (US$1,131.80) a metric tonne by the midday break.
The contract has declined 0.51 per cent so far this week.
The market traded lower on weaker crude oil and soybean oil prices during Asian hours, said David Ng, a proprietary trader at Kuala Lumpur-based trading firm Iceberg X Sdn Bhd.
Dalian's most-active soyoil contract rose 0.39 per cent, while its palm oil contract added 1.12 per cent. Soyoil prices on the Chicago Board of Trade were down 0.2 per cent.
Palm oil tracks the price movements of rival edible oils, as it competes for a share of the global vegetable oils market.
Oil prices fell on optimism that the Middle East conflict could be nearing an end after a 10-day ceasefire between Lebanon and Israel took effect and President Donald Trump said the US and Iran may meet for talks on the weekend.
Weaker crude oil futures make palm a less attractive option for biodiesel feedstock.
The ringgit, palm's currency of trade, weakened 0.03 per cent against the dollar, making the commodity slightly cheaper for buyers holding foreign currencies.
Malaysia's palm oil–based biodiesel consumption is set to rise by more than 300,000 tonnes annually, the Malaysian Palm Oil Board said, as the country joins top producer Indonesia in raising blending mandates to reduce reliance on energy imports.
Palm oil may break a resistance at RM4,517 per tonne and rise into a range of RM4,566-RM4,584, Reuters technical analyst Wang Tao said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Jail me': Widower pleads for prison after admitting to cable theft
Article:
BATU PAHAT: A 50-year-old widower told the Magistrate's Court here today that he preferred to be sent to prison after pleading guilty to cable theft.
Azman Abdullah made the plea before Magistrate Arun Noval Dass after being charged under Section 431A of the Penal Code for committing mischief by damaging cables.
"I ask for a prison sentence," he said.
The court sentenced the father of three to four months' jail, effective from the date of his arrest on April 15.
The oil palm plantation labourer committed the offence at Kampung Bunagong, Ayer Hitam, at 12.30am on April 15.
Section 431A carries a maximum penalty of two years' imprisonment, a fine, or both, upon conviction.
According to the facts of the case, a complainant lodged a police report after spotting a man riding a bicycle in a suspicious manner while carrying electricity cables.
The accused attempted to flee when he noticed the complainant but was apprehended and handed over to the police.
Following the arrest, police seized the cut cables along with a hacksaw, wire cutters, pliers, and a wood saw used in the crime.
Deputy Public Prosecutor Firdaus Ruslan prosecuted, while the accused was represented by National Legal Aid Foundation lawyer Noormaahani Rais.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RTD to probe viral bus driver clip, action to follow
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: The Road Transport Department (RTD) will verify the authenticity and circumstances surrounding a newly surfaced viral video allegedly showing a bus driver behaving in an unsafe manner before determining its next course of action.
The clip, which emerged a day after another incident involving a bus driver drew widespread attention online, has sparked renewed public concern over road safety and enforcement.
RTD director-general Datuk Aedy Fadly Ramli said they would first establish the facts of the case, noting that investigations into such incidents are often conducted in coordination with the police, depending on the elements involved.
"I need to verify the matter first. For cases like this, we will usually conduct investigations together with the police.
"If it involves criminal elements, accidents or matters under police jurisdiction, the case will be handled by the police first," he said.
He was here to introduce the special 'P-P' vehicle registration number series in conjunction with RTD's 80th anniversary celebration.
Aedy explained that RTD would step in where offences fall within the scope of the Road Transport Act and do not involve criminal aspects.
"For cases under RTD's jurisdiction, particularly those related to the Road Transport Act without criminal elements, we can carry out our own investigations.
"There is a consensus between RTD and the police on how such cases are handled," he added.
Aedy said the department would take action if violations of road transport laws are established following investigations.
The back-to-back viral videos have intensified scrutiny on bus driver conduct, with authorities expected to determine whether enforcement or legal action is warranted.
Police launched an investigation after discovering the 80-second clip of the incident, uploaded at 1.05pm.
Alor Gajah police chief Superintendent Ahmad Abu Bakar said the footage showed the
driver reclining in his seat
with only one hand on the steering wheel while driving at night.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: School dropout arrested over teen's rape and sexual assault
Article:
BATU PAHAT: A 17-year-old school dropout has been remanded for seven days to assist in investigations into the alleged rape and sexual assault of a 14-year-old girl he met two days prior.
Magistrate Nurasidah A Rahman issued the remand order, which expires next Thursday, following the suspect's arrest yesterday.
The case is being investigated under Section 376 of the Penal Code for rape and Section 14 (a) of the Sexual Offences Against Children Act. Both offences carry lengthy jail terms and whipping upon conviction.
The suspect, who works at a window tinting shop, reportedly befriended the victim through WhatsApp before the pair went out together.
He allegedly took the victim to his workplace and committed the offences at 1.40am yesterday.
The matter came to light when the victim returned home in tears and told her mother about the incident.
The mother lodged a police report, leading to the suspect's arrest at a house shortly after.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 10 witnesses, fresh evidence as Zara Qairina inquest gathers pace
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: More than 10 witnesses are expected to be called in the next phase of the inquest into the death of Zara Qairina Mahathir.
Lawyer Rizwandean M. Borhan said the 70th witness, Assistant Superintendent Mohd Zaidi Abu Hassan, will continue his testimony when proceedings resume next month.
The police officer, who conducted forensic analysis on 15 mobile phones linked to the case, has yet to complete his testimony on the 67th day of the inquest.
"The examination by the conducting officer has been ongoing since yesterday. He has tendered the contents of his analysis, which include WhatsApp chats, videos and audio recordings," Rizwandean told a press conference.
[[nid:1419806]]
"His examination is still incomplete and will continue in the next session on May 18," he said, adding that his legal team has yet to cross-examine the witness.
Rizwandean is among the lawyers representing Zara's mother, Noraidah Lamat.
The court has fixed May 18 to 22 and June 22 to 26 for the next hearings.
Consultant forensic psychiatrist Dr Chua Sze Hung of Hospital Mesra Bukit Padang is expected to be recalled next month for further examination.
The defence is also expected to call expert witnesses, including document examiner Dr Linthini Gannetion, handwriting and document examiners Dr Tay Eue Kam and Dr Dzulkiflee Ismail, and forensic scientist Dr Wan Nur Syuhaila Mat Desa.
Additional experts expected to testify include child psychologist Dr Noor Aishah Rosli and psychiatrist Dr Wong Haw Huo.
Rizwandean said the team has informed coroner Amir Shah Amir Hassan that around 10 to 11 witnesses may be called, including a child, several relatives of the deceased – among them a cousin – as well as Zara's mother.
Earlier in proceedings, when examined by coordinating officer Sofia S. Samayan, Mohd Zaidi, 44, presented WhatsApp messages from one of the mobile phones submitted as evidence in court.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia-India ties built on respect, understanding and friendship, says MP
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A member of parliament has hailed the enduring ties between Malaysia and India, saying they are built on long-standing mutual respect, understanding and friendship.
Segamat MP Yuneswaran Ramaraj said this at the 15th anniversary celebration of the Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose Indian Cultural Centre (NSCBICC) here recently.
Yuneswaran commended the centre for its role in promoting Indian cultural heritage and strengthening people-to-people ties between the two nations over the past 15 years.
The event was graced by Tunku Zain Al-'Abidin Tuanku Muhriz and attended by Indian High Commissioner to Malaysia B.N. Reddy.
Guests were treated to a series of performances, including rhythmic yoga demonstrations and classical Bharatanatyam dance.
Yuneswaran said such displays of cultural richness serve as a vital bridge between communities.
He expressed hope that this spirit of unity would continue to be nurtured to ensure a more harmonious and united future.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ericsson slightly lags profit expectations as AI demand drives up chip costs
Article:
Sweden's Ericsson reported a first-quarter core profit that slightly missed market expectations on Friday, citing increasing chip costs caused by artificial intelligence demand and a sales slowdown in North America.
The company reported an adjusted operating profit of 5.2 billion Swedish crowns (US$566 million), excluding restructuring charges, for the first quarter of 2026. Analysts polled by Infront were expecting 5.4 billion crowns on average.
Ericsson, one of the main Western suppliers of network equipment alongside Finland's Nokia, is betting heavily on the US market even as transatlantic ties have become strained under President Donald Trump's rule.
The Swedish group has significant exposure to the United States, especially after winning a US$14 billion deal with operator AT&T in 2023, which could help outweigh slower telecoms investments in other markets.
"We are facing increasing input costs, especially in semiconductors, caused in part by AI demand," CEO Börje Ekholm said in a statement.
The company reported first-quarter net sales of 49.3 billion crowns, compared with an Infront poll estimate of 50.7 billion crowns.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RTD not ruling out insiders in alleged unauthorised '111' plate transfer
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: The Road Transport Department (RTD) is investigating complaints regarding the alleged transfer of unique vehicle registration numbers without the consent of original owners.
RTD director-general Datuk Aedy Fadly Ramli did not rule out the possibility of insider involvement but cautioned against jumping to conclusions.
"If there are allegations, we will investigate and take action based on the procedures and laws in place. RTD will look into each case in detail," he said at the launch of the special PP vehicle registration series.
Aedy said while the department is committed to assisting those affected, any decision to reverse ownership or take enforcement action would depend on the investigation findings.
"We will assist, but it depends on the ownership transfer process itself. We need to determine whether the transfer was done in accordance with the law.
"In some cases, the claims may not be accurate. There are situations where the transfer was legitimately carried out, but the complainant believes otherwise," he said.
He said factors such as misunderstandings over ownership status, incomplete documentation, or procedural issues could contribute to disputes.
"It is not necessarily due to data theft. We need to examine whether the procedures for transfer of ownership were properly followed, or if there were weaknesses in documentation or verification," he said.
Aedy confirmed that reports had been lodged but did not provide specific figures. He said RTD would scrutinise transaction records to ensure compliance and strengthen processes to prevent system abuse.
Yesterday, a businessman claimed he lost ownership of his luxury vehicle and high-value registration plate after both were transferred without his consent.
The victim, who wished to be known only as Mr Tan, said his
Toyota Alphard
and its registration plate bearing the number "111" were transferred without his knowledge.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RTD rolls out 'P-P' plate series to mark 80 years, launches discount sale
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: The Road Transport Department (RTD) will introduce a special "P-P" vehicle registration number series to mark its 80th anniversary.
Director-general Datuk Aedy Fadly Ramli said the exclusive series symbolises eight decades of the department's role in shaping the country's transport system.
"The introduction of this special series reflects RTD's identity, legacy and evolution as a leading agency in road transport management, enforcement and regulation," he said at a press conference at The Top Komtar on Friday.
The "P-P" series will be offered to the public via an online bidding process through the JPJ eBid system, which Aedy said ensures transparency and efficiency.
Bidding opens on April 20 and closes on April 24 at 10pm, with results to be announced within 24 hours on April 25. Successful bidders must register their numbers on a vehicle within 12 months from the date of the official results letter.
Aedy said the "P-P" prefix was chosen for its association with Penang, where the anniversary celebrations will be held, as well as its symbolic link to the department's transport and enforcement functions.
He added that previous technical issues, including lag during bidding, had been resolved following system upgrades.
"The improved JPJ eBid application has addressed earlier complaints, and we are no longer receiving significant reports of disruptions," he said.
Separately, Aedy announced that nearly 50,000 unsold registration numbers would be offered at a 50 percent discount during the Karnival Mega JPJ Madani, taking place from April 24 to 26 at Stadium Batu Kawan.
"These numbers, including attractive category plates priced as low as RM250, will only be available for purchase on-site on April 26," he said.
The public has been invited to attend the carnival, with the full list of available numbers to be released later.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hospital accused of 'license abuse', cover-up as staff cancer cluster emerges
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Allegations of negligence and a systemic cover-up on radiation safety at a hospital have sparked outrage, with a former staff member claiming safety lapses have created a "cancer cluster" among personnel.
The former staff, identified only as Fauziah, is a former radiographer at the hospital's Nuclear Medicine Unit who is now battling Stage 4 Hodgkin Lymphoma.
She alleged that at least seven staff members have fallen victim to chronic illnesses, including lung, blood, and breast cancers, as well as severe thyroid issues.
At a Press conference today organised by #ReformIPT, she shared evidence on the alleged safety lapses at the hospital.
Among the documents presented was an official report from the Ministry of Health's Medical Radiation Control division (BKRP).
The report, signed by its director Basri Sapi concluded that the hospital's facilities were "safe" and in full compliance with the Atomic Energy Licensing Act 1984 (Act 304).
Fauziah, however, alleged these findings directly contradicted a prior online meeting with four BKRP enforcement officers on Feb 14.
"In that meeting, the officers admitted to me that there were clear SOP (standard operating procedure) breaches within the unit.
"Yet, when the final report was signed, everything was suddenly fine. This is a blatant manipulation of technical data to keep the public in the dark," Fauziah said.
She further alleged that a second "secret" visit was conducted by BKRP officials without the knowledge of the hospital's corporate management.
"I have the agenda for that follow-up meeting. It's title explicitly mentions 'non-compliance with licensing'. If there was no breach, why was there a meeting about non-compliance?"
She also revealed dangerous "job-scope creeping", saying radiographers were forced to perform specialised "radiopharmacy" duties.
"When I questioned the radiation protection officer (RPO) if this was my job, I was told to just do whatever work was assigned.
"Only after I raised the issue did they finally hire a radiopharmacist," she said.
Fauziah further alleged that staff preparing Gamma-emitting isotopes were denied full Personal Protective Equipment (PPE).
"We were given basic lead shielding that only covered parts of the body. Gamma radiation penetrates the entire body. It is no wonder staff are falling ill," she said, citing the 'stochastic effect", which is long-term cancer risks from repeated radiation exposure.
A video footage was also screened during the Press conference which showed a chaotic flow within the unit's "Control Area", where radioactive patients mingled with the public.
"Once a patient is injected, they emit radiation. In this hospital, they are squeezed into a small unit and then take public lifts, potentially exposing hundreds of unsuspecting visitors to accumulated doses of radiation daily," Fauziah warned.
Fauziah claimed the hospital may have "abused" an old license originally issued for a different wing to bypass mandatory safety inspections required for its newer facility.
"If they used an old license for a new location, it means the necessary infrastructure audits were never conducted. This is a direct violation of Act 304," she added.
#ReformIPT reiterated their demand for an immediate independent audit and full transparency, asserting that the "culture of silence" within the hospital's management is costing lives and compromising medical safety at a major public institution.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Jail and RM5k fine for bus driver, girlfriend in reckless driving case
Article:
JASIN: A bus driver caught on camera allowing his girlfriend to sit on his lap while driving has been sentenced to one year in jail for reckless driving.
Arif Fahmi Abd Salam, 36, was also fined RM5,000 and disqualified from holding or obtaining a driving licence for five years.
His girlfriend, Nur Adila Najwa Ilham Ameerrullah, 21, was sentenced to six months in jail and fined RM5,000.
She was also disqualified from holding a driving licence for five years.
Magistrate Mazana Sinin ordered the pair, who pleaded guilty, to serve an additional two months in jail if they fail to pay the fines.
Arif and Nur Adila were charged under Section 42(1) of the Road Transport Act for reckless and dangerous driving.
The case went viral on Monday after an eight-second video circulated on social media showing a bus travelling along the North-South Expressway.
In the clip, believed to have been recorded by a passenger, the woman is seen handling the steering wheel while sitting on the driver's lap as the bus was in motion.
A separate video, purportedly captured via dashcam from a trailing vehicle, also surfaced online showing the same bus swerving through traffic.
The driver, who was employed by Sri Maju Group, has since been sacked.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Activists mulling legal action against hospital for alleged radiation exposure
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A hospital in the Klang Valley is facing potential legal action over alleged negligence in
radiation safety protocols
, which is alleged to have put both healthcare workers and patients at grave risk.
The New Straits Times is withholding the name of the hospital pending comment from its management and the Higher Education Ministry.
During a press briefing today, a representative from AIMI & EZ Advocates & Solicitors, said while radiation exposure is an inherent risk in nuclear medicine, it must be managed as a "controlled risk".
Drawing an analogy to a construction site, the representative Siti Nur Sabrina Mohammad said just as employers must mandate safety helmets, the hospital has a legal duty to provide continuous monitoring.
"The risk was foreseeable and inevitable. The failure to implement reasonable safety measures constitutes a clear case of negligence," she said, adding that the lapses pose a broader threat to public health.
#ReformIPT secretariat representative Brendon Gan said there is a reasonable "cause of action" for the case to be brought to court.
"While we cannot turn back time on the health of Fauziah and other affected staff, we demand full accountability.
"If this is not exposed, many more will suffer long-term impacts," Gan added.
At the Press conference today, Fauziah, a Stage 4 cancer survivor, presented documented proof of mishandling of their standard operating procedures (SOP) during PET-CT scanner installations.
The group also said they are working closely with Lembah Pantai MP Datuk Seri Fahmi Fadzil to escalate the evidence to the Ministry of Health (KKM) for federal intervention and a thorough investigation into the hospital's management of radioactive equipment.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump characterises Iran war as a 'little diversion'
Article:
WASHINGTON: President Donald Trump said Thursday the US war with Iran was a "little diversion" during his second term in power, as recent polling shows the conflict's unpopularity with the American public.
At an event in Las Vegas, Nevada, touting his "no tax on tips" measure from the major tax reform bill passed last year, the 79-year-old president boasted about his economic record since returning to office in 2025.
"We had the best economy in the history of our country in my first term. And we're blowing it out now... And despite our little diversion to the lovely country of Iran, lovely place," Trump told the crowd of supporters.
"But we had to do that, because otherwise, bad things could happen, the really bad thing," he continued, referencing Iran's apparent nuclear potential.
An Ipsos poll conducted last weekend found 51 per cent of the more than 1,000 respondents thought the Iran war was not worth the costs associated with it.
Less than a quarter of respondents, 24 per cent, said the opposite.
Another recent poll published Wednesday by Quinnipiac University found 65 per cent of US voters blame Trump for the recent rise in gas prices prompted by the Strait of Hormuz closing since the start of the Iran war.
The same poll reported only 36 per cent of voters approved of how Trump was handling the situation with Iran, while 58 per cent disapproved.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MPOC secures RM47.5m potential palm oil sales from Middle East trade mission
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Palm Oil Council (MPOC) has strengthened Malaysia's push into emerging markets following the successful conclusion of a trade networking visit involving 35 buyers from Central Asian Republics.
The initiative underscores efforts to expand Malaysia's footprint in high-growth regions, particularly as demand continues to rise across food manufacturing industries such as confectionery, ice cream, bakery and margarine, where palm-based ingredients are increasingly used.
The visiting delegation comprised manufacturers from key segments such as confectionery, ice cream, bakery, and margarine as industries that are increasingly adopting palm-based solutions.
A key highlight of the programme was the BizMatch session, which facilitated 352 structured business meetings between 18 Malaysian suppliers and the visiting buyers.
The engagements generated an estimated RM47.5 million in potential sales, signalling robust commercial interest and strengthening Central Asia's position as a promising export market.
MPOC chief executive officer Belvinder Sron said the region presents strong growth opportunities, particularly in higher-value segments such as speciality fats and food manufacturing applications.
"The strong engagement observed during this visit reflects increasing confidence among buyers and the readiness of Malaysian suppliers to support this market," she added.
Additionally, the programme included visits to plantations to observe harvesting operations and tours of advanced bulking facilities in Johor Bahru as well as engagement sessions with industry players who offered insights into downstream capabilities.
The event also drew support from Uzbekistan's ambassador to Malaysia, Karomidin Gadoev, who engaged with both buyers and suppliers.
He noted that Uzbekistan and the wider Central Asian region present significant opportunities for Malaysian palm oil and encouraged Malaysian suppliers to establish a stronger presence in the region, including positioning stocks within Uzbekistan to enhance distribution efficiency.
He added that the government of Uzbekistan stands firmly in support of such initiatives through trade facilitation measures and infrastructure support.
MPOC also noted strong participation from Malaysian industry players, with suppliers showing keen interest in expanding into Central Asia through direct engagement and tailored product offerings.
The strong response from both buyers and suppliers underscores the potential for long-term collaboration, positioning Central Asia as an increasingly important market in Malaysia's broader export diversification strategy.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia inflation rises 1.7pct in March on higher transport and services costs
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's inflation rose 1.7 per cent to 136.4 in March compared to 134.1 last year, according to the Statistics Department.
Chief Statistician Datuk Seri Mohd Uzir Mahidin said that Malaysia's inflation last month was mainly driven by an increase in the transport group to 1.6 per cent from a decline of 0.7 per cent in February.
The rise in inflation was also contributed to by increases in personal care, social protection and miscellaneous goods and services, which rose seven per cent.
Across the groups, the insurance and financial services sector rose to 4.9 per cent from February, alcoholic beverages and tobacco climbed to 2.7 per cent, restaurant and accommodation services inched higher to 2.6 per cent, health climbed to 1.4 per cent, and information and communication rose to 1.4 per cent. Additionally, housing, water, electricity, gas and other fuels climbed to 1.2 per cent, while the recreation, sport and culture sector rose to 1.0 per cent.
Conversely, education moderated to 2.5 per cent, food and beverages dropped to 1.1 per cent, and furnishings, household equipment and routine household maintenance slipped to 0.1 per cent compared to the previous month.
The monthly headline inflation in March increased to 0.3 per cent as compared to 0.2 per cent in February, while for the first quarter, inflation rose to 1.6 per cent compared to 1.3 per cent in the fourth quarter of 2025.
DOSM data showed that 62.5 per cent of items, or 358 out of 573 items, recorded price increases.
Nonetheless, out of this total, 348 items or 97.2 per cent registered an increase of less than or equal to 10 per cent, while only 10 items recorded increases of more than 10 per cent in March.
The remaining 171 items showed a decline, and 44 items remained unchanged.
The food and beverages group, which contributes 29.8 per cent of the total consumer price index (CPI), posted an inflation rate at 1.1 per cent in March.
Expenditure classes of food at home, namely fish and other seafood as well as meat, also eased in March.
The expenditure class of meat eased to 0.9 per cent in March as compared to 1.7 per cent in February. This was contributed to by pork and duck, which rose to 3.4 per cent and 0.6 per cent.
Chicken, as the largest component in the expenditure class of meat, dropped to 0.3 per cent as compared to 2.5 per cent registered in February.
Inflation for the group of housing, water, electricity, gas and other fuels increased to 1.2 per cent in March as compared to 1.1 per cent in February.
At the state level, eight states recorded increases above the national inflation level (1.7 per cent), namely Pahang (2.5 per cent), Labuan (2.4 per cent), Negeri Sembilan (2.2 per cent), Kuala Lumpur (2.2 per cent), Sabah (2.1 per cent), Johor (2.0 per cent), Kedah (1.9 per cent) and Penang (1.8 per cent).
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: LNG prices to track oil price trends in Asia amid ongoing diplomacy: Rystad Energy
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Global liquefied natural gas (LNG) price movements, especially in Asia, are expected to continue tracking oil price trends, as oil fuel switching remains prevalent in more price-sensitive countries, according to Norway-based energy research company Rystad Energy.
Its research analyst Mathieu Utting said in a note that despite the United States and Iran walking away from talks in Pakistan last weekend without reaching a peace agreement, ongoing diplomatic discussions between both parties have helped calm global LNG prices.
"The ongoing diplomatic discussions between both have calmed global LNG prices, with Asia LNG derivatives price for June having decreased 14 per cent week-over-week to US$16.50 per million British thermal units (MMBtu) this week.
"Although Asia could see some supply relief with LNG Canada increasing shipments, Australia nearly fully recovered from the previous cyclone outages and Golden Pass expected to ship its first export cargo by the end of the month," he said.
However, the downside correction in prices could be overstated, as recovery in West Asian LNG supply may take longer than expected, while the traffic outlook through the Strait of Hormuz remains uncertain.
Similarly, it said previously inflated European gas prices due to perceived geopolitical risks have also seen some downward correction, with the Title Transfer Facility (TTF) falling to US$14.30 per MMBtu on April 15, its lowest level since the beginning of the war.
"However, this reflects a well-supplied European continent with US LNG and continued strong pipeline imports as demand fundamentals remain weak on above-normal temperatures," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Vale appoints Edwin Gerard as CEO of Malaysia unit
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Global mining giant Vale S.A. has appointed Edwin Gerard as chief executive officer of its Malaysian subsidiary, Vale Malaysia Minerals Sdn Bhd, marking a leadership transition as the group strengthens its long-term strategic footprint in the country.
Gerard succeeds Leonardo Paiva, under whose leadership Vale expanded its community-focused initiatives in Perak, including urban rejuvenation programmes in Manjung that supported the revitalisation of Beruas and Manjung Lama. The company also introduced the Teluk Rubiah Scholarship Programme to nurture high-potential local talent.
Since commencing operations in Teluk Rubiah in 2014, Vale has invested approximately RM80 million in social development initiatives, reinforcing its long-standing commitment to community development alongside industrial growth.
In his new role, Gerard will oversee one of Vale's key strategic hubs in Asia, the Teluk Rubiah Maritime Terminal in Perak, which handles around 20 million tonnes of iron ore annually. The facility serves as a critical distribution centre for Vale's Asia-Pacific operations and remains central to the group's focus on safe, reliable and sustainable operations.
"Malaysia continues to play an important role in Vale's Asia Pacific operations, and we remain firmly committed to strengthening our long-term presence and contribution in the country. In this role, I aim to continue building on the strong foundation established over the years by deepening partnerships with local stakeholders, enhancing collaboration across the ecosystem and ensuring the consistent delivery of safe, reliable and sustainable operations that create lasting value," Gerard said.
Gerard brings more than 25 years of international experience in large-scale port operations, logistics and infrastructure management, having held senior leadership roles across Oman, Nigeria, Egypt, Turkey and Malaysia. His most recent posting was as the chief operating officer of Johor Port Bhd.
Notably, he is the first Malaysian appointed to lead Vale Malaysia, reflecting the group's broader strategy of developing and empowering the local talent within its global operations.
Today, Vale's Malaysian operations are supported by a workforce of over 95 per cent Malaysian, spanning the Teluk Rubiah Maritime Terminal in Perak, which represents the largest single foreign direct investment in the state, and its Asia Pacific corporate office in Kuala Lumpur.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Contractor charged with using forged documents to secure govt contracts
Article:
JOHOR BARU: A contractor has pleaded not guilty at the Sessions Court to six counts of using forged documents to secure government tenders.
Mohd Safri Ibrahim, 47, claimed trial to all charges read before judge Nor Aziati Jaafar.
He was accused of dishonestly using forged quotations and bank statements as genuine to obtain government contracts for his company.
The offences were allegedly committed at the Muar Public Works Department and the Muar Drainage and Irrigation Department between Aug 27, 2019 and Feb 9, 2021.
The charges were framed under Section 471 of the Penal Code. The offence is punishable by a maximum two-year prison sentence, a fine, or both upon conviction.
Deputy public prosecutor Syairah Aqilah Khalil prosecuted, while the accused was represented by lawyer Ahmad Hafiz A. Bakar.
The prosecution requested bail of RM6,000 for each charge.
However, Ahmad Hafiz pleaded for a lower amount as his client is unemployed and has to support three school-going children and an ailing mother.
The court allowed bail at RM24,000 for all charges and fixed May 15 for mention.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Abandoned toddler: authorities race to trace missing mother
Article:
KEPALA BATAS: Welfare authorities in Penang are seeking to locate the biological mother and relatives of a three-year-six-month-old girl believed to be a victim of neglect.
In a statement, the Social Welfare Department (JKM) of Seberang Perai Utara (SPU) said the child, identified as Amanda Mikayla Abdullah, was allegedly left by her mother with a caretaker, Layla Yamna Abdul Hajar, without any identification documents.
"Investigations by the Child Protector of the SPU District JKM found that the child's biological mother, Nurul Izzati Mohamad A'sri, 29, cannot be contacted and has yet to be located.
"The mother's last known address was in Yan, and she is believed to be working in Penang," the statement said.
Members of the public with information on the child's family are urged to contact SPU District Child Protector Norpatin Bakar at 04-5758715 or 04-5758716, or via email at norpatin@jkm.gov.my.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Dead fish found at Sungai Ketil raise pollution fears
Article:
GUA MUSANG: Hundreds of dead fish found at Taman Rimba Sungai Ketil this morning have sparked health and safety concerns among visitors at the popular recreational spot.
Angler Muhammad Che Mahmood, 29, found carcasses scattered along the riverbank about 7.45am.
Muhammad said he arrived early to fish but found the area deserted and the water unusually murky and dark, which he initially attributed to heavy rain the previous night.
He saw small bubbles on the surface and realised they were fish gasping for air. He checked about 20 metres along the bank and found hundreds of dead fish washed up.
He identified the species as baung, sebarau, tengas, and kenerat, noting that most were juveniles.
He said he was disappointed and saddened that the fish were likely killed by irresponsible acts, forcing him to cancel his fishing plans.
Another visitor, Muhammad Afif Sukhairi, 28, expressed concern over possible pollution, noting that the site is a frequent weekend destination for families due to its proximity to Gua Musang town.
He said the area is usually crowded with people bathing and picnicking early in the day, but was quiet, likely due to the murky water. He added the fish appeared to have died from a lack of oxygen and warned it could indicate pollution that may endanger visitors' health.
Gua Musang district officer Mohd Roshdi Ismail said he had been informed of the incident.
He said the matter would be referred to the Department of Environment and the state Fisheries Department.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysian vessel Ocean Thunder enters Singapore waters after return from Iran
Article:
KOTA TINGGI: Malaysian crude oil tanker Ocean Thunder has entered Singapore waters and is on its final approach to Pengerang, with vessel tracking data indicating an arrival time of 1.30pm today.
Automatic Identification System (AIS) updates show the Panama-flagged very large crude carrier (VLCC) is underway at 7.1 knots on a steady course of 66 degrees, threading through the Singapore Strait.
The 274-metre vessel, drawing 17 metres, is carrying approximately one million barrels of Iraqi Basrah Heavy crude destined for the Pengerang Integrated Petroleum Complex (PIPC), a downstream hub operated by Petroliam Nasional Bhd (Petronas).
Its presence in the strait, the final gateway before Malaysian waters, signals that berthing and discharge operations are imminent, subject to port traffic and clearance.
The delivery follows a high-stakes voyage that began in Iraq in early March.
The tanker
transited
the Strait of Hormuz on April 5, a critical chokepoint under strain amid tensions linked to the United States–Israel–Iran conflict.
Only a handful of Malaysia-linked vessels have made the crossing in recent weeks due to the fragile state of global energy supply routes.
The Ocean Thunder is understood to be among a limited number granted passage following diplomatic engagements. Iran reportedly allowed transit for ships carrying Iraqi crude, which was exempted from restrictions.
Basrah Heavy, a dense and high-sulphur crude, is typically processed at complex refineries such as the PIPC to produce higher-value petroleum products.
Shipping intelligence indicates only one other Malaysia-bound tanker, the Serifos, has completed a similar Hormuz transit since tensions escalated.
New Straits Times journalists are stationed outside the PIPC, a high-security facility with restricted access.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mother, daughter among three charged over robbery, assault in Penang
Article:
GEORGETOWN: A mother and daughter were among three individuals charged in a Sessions Court here today with robbery and causing injury to a man.
The accused – G Thananjayan, 41; Nur Fadzlin Mohamed Salim, 27; and her mother Azura Abdul Latiff, 42 – pleaded not guilty after the charges were read before Judge Juraidah Abbas.
They are accused of committing robbery and intentionally causing injury to a 32-year-old man at a condominium in Batu Ferringhi around 5:30am on April 13.
The charges were framed under Section 394 of the Penal Code, read together with Section 34, which carries a penalty of up to 20 years' imprisonment, as well as a possible fine or whipping upon conviction.
Deputy public prosecutor Anuarul Hakim Abdul Muen led the prosecution, while the trio were represented by lawyer A Mahesh.
The prosecution proposed bail at RM8,000 for each accused. However, the defence sought a reduction, citing their clients' financial circumstances.
The 41-year-old works at a recycling facility, the 27-year-old is a clinic assistant, while the mother is unemployed.
The defence also argued that all three are based in the state, pose no flight risk, and had cooperated with investigators.
The court set bail at RM4,000 each with one local surety, and imposed an additional condition barring the accused from contacting the victim or any witnesses until the case concludes.
The case has been fixed for mention on June 3 pending a medical report.
The victim is understood to be the boyfriend of the 27-year-old accused, while the male co-accused is said to be a friend of her mother.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia economy grows 5.3pct in Q1 2026, underpinned by resilient manufacturing
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's economy expanded by 5.3 per cent in the first quarter of 2026, according to advance estimates released by the Department of Statistics Malaysia (DOSM), moderating from the 6.3 per cent growth recorded in the previous quarter.
Chief statistician Datuk Seri Mohd Uzir Mahidin said that the first quarter reflected an economy that was "fundamentally resilient", even with the rising global uncertainties, particularly elevated oil prices following the geopolitical tensions.
The manufacturing sector sustained positive momentum in the first two months of 2026, with output expanding by 7.3 per cent and 4.2 per cent, respectively, driven largely by export-oriented industries.
A review of sectoral performance showed a moderation in growth momentum across the five main sectors in the first quarter.
The services sector continued to anchor overall expansion, registering a 5.4 per cent growth attributed to the growth mainly in the wholesale and retail trade sub-sector.
DOSM said this reflected sustained consumer spending, supported by a stable labour market, higher household income and ongoing people-centric initiatives to stimulate household-related activities.
The information and communication sub-sector also contributed to the performance in this quarter, supported by the rising demand for data centres and generative AI-related activities.
The manufacturing sector posted a growth of 5.8 per cent, supported by strong output in the electrical, electronic and optical products as well as the vegetable and animal oils and fats as well as food processing.
"The construction sector eased in this quarter at 7.8 per cent, supported primarily by specialised construction activities and non-residential buildings. The agriculture sector grew 2.8 per cent, attributed to the growth in oil palm and livestock sub-sectors," said Mohd Uzir.
Meanwhile, the mining and quarrying sector declined to 1.1 per cent from 2.0 per cent growth in the last quarter, influenced by lower production, particularly in crude oil, condensate and natural gas.
Seasonal festive spending during Chinese New Year, alongside preparations for Ramadan and Hari Raya Aidilfitri, continued to support overall economic activities.
DOSM said the disbursement of Sumbangan Asas Rahmah and the second phase of civil servant salary revisions contributed to a steady growth in distributive trade sales, which increased by 7.3 per cent in January and 5.3 per cent in February 2026.
On the external front, trade activity remained supported by continued expansion in both domestic exports and re-exports.
Subsequent to these advance estimates, the preliminary GDP data for the first quarter will be released on May 15.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Energy crisis demands focus on food security, says Akmal Nasrullah
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The global energy crisis requires the government to prioritise food security, says Economy Minister Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir.
He said the primary challenge currently facing the plantation and agriculture sectors is the rising cost of fertiliser, which has driven up overall production costs.
"How do we ensure our farmers remain profitable? When we examine the input for agriculture, there are specific factors that contribute to the actual production cost.
"One example is fuel, specifically diesel, and the second is fertiliser. This is why fertiliser was among the earliest issues discussed by the National Economic Action Council (MTEN) to ensure supply sustainability."
Akmal was speaking to reporters after visiting the People's Income Initiative-Agropreneur (IPR-INTAN) project.
He said the urea fertiliser used in Malaysian farms is derived from domestic petroleum sources, and other materials, including nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium compounds and phosphates, still need to be imported.
He added that fertiliser supply was a key area of cooperation discussed during Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese's visit to Malaysia, which concluded yesterday.
"The assurance is that they will assist our country in increasing the supply of phosphates to Malaysia," he said.
On the IPR-INTAN programme, Akmal Nasrullah said it has produced 2,300 participants to date, with a cumulative harvest value reaching RM22 million.
He said the ministry aims to recruit between 500 and 600 new participants for the programme this year.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bus driver, girlfriend get one‑year and six‑month jail terms in viral ‘lap‑driving’ case
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 17 — An express bus driver and his girlfriend were sentenced to jail today after pleading guilty in the Magistrates’ Court to dangerous driving last week.
Sinar Harian
reported that Arif Fahmi Abd Salam, 36, and Nur Adila Najwa Ilham Ameerrullah, 21, entered their pleas before Magistrate Mazana Sinin.
Arif Fahmi received a one‑year jail term, while Nur Adila Najwa was sentenced to six months’ imprisonment, with both also fined RM5,000.
Prosecutors said the pair jointly drove a bus bearing registration number APE 9588 in a manner dangerous to the public.
The offence occurred last Sunday at about 7.30pm at the exit of the Bemban Rest and Service Area (R&R) along the southbound North‑South Expressway in the Jasin district.
They were charged under Section 42(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987 (Amendment) 2020 read together with Section 34 of the Penal Code.
The law provides for up to five years’ imprisonment, a fine of between RM5,000 and RM15,000, and disqualification from holding a driving licence for at least five years upon conviction.
Nur Adila Najwa also faced a separate charge under Section 14 of the Minor Offences Act 1955 for using abusive and insulting words against a 44‑year‑old woman, causing public annoyance.
The offence was committed the same day at about 6pm inside the bus as it exited the Bemban R&R southbound in Jasin.
The charge carries a maximum fine of RM100.
Both accused, who were unrepresented, sought leniency on the grounds that this was their first offence and that no accident occurred and passengers arrived safely at their destination.
Arif Fahmi told the court he had already suffered consequences for his actions after being immediately terminated by his employer, leaving him without income to support his mother, who has hypertension, and his two younger siblings.
He also said he must attend interviews with the Road Transport Department (JPJ) and the Land Public Transport Agency (APAD) regarding the same case.
Nur Adila Najwa, speaking tearfully, asked for a light sentence because she is young and hopes to continue her studies.
“I plead guilty. I am only 21 years old and have lived with my grandmother since I was a baby,” she said.
“I obtained 8As and 2Bs in SPM but had to decline an offer to pursue further studies due to financial constraints,” she added.
She said she moved to Kuala Lumpur to work after her grandmother died and hopes to resume her education.
Deputy Public Prosecutor Mohd Asri Abd Rahman Noor Syazwani Mohamad Yusri urged the court to impose a deterrent sentence due to the seriousness of the case.
He said the court must prioritise public interest and send a clear message to the public.
“This case caused concern among public transport users after it went viral on social media, as it clearly showed that safety was ignored while operating a large vehicle,” he said.
“There were 27 lives on board the bus and the accused’s actions were unacceptable,” he added.
He also informed the court that the first accused’s driving licence had expired on April 1, 2026, before the incident on April 12.
The court ordered the jail terms to take effect from the date of conviction and imposed an additional two‑month jail term if the fines are not paid.
Both accused were also disqualified from holding a driving licence for five years.
Nur Adila Najwa was additionally fined RM100 after pleading guilty to the charge under the Minor Offences Act.
Mazana said the pair had clearly disregarded safety despite no accident occurring and expressed hope that the sentence would serve as a lesson.

Title: JPJ probes claims of vehicle number plates being transferred without owners’ knowledge
Article:
GEORGE TOWN, April 17 — The Road Transport Department (JPJ) has confirmed receiving several complaints regarding alleged transfers of ownership of unique vehicle registration numbers without the original owners’ knowledge.
JPJ director-general Datuk Aedy Fadly Ramli said the department will conduct detailed investigations before taking any action.
“Any reports received would be investigated in accordance with existing procedures and legal provisions to verify the validity of the claims,” he told reporters after launching a special vehicle registration number series at The Top here today.
“There have been several complaints related to ownership transfers carried out without the consent of the original owner,” he added.
He said JPJ will carry out investigations and take appropriate action based on existing procedures and laws.
He said JPJ will assist affected owners, but any further action will depend on the procedures of the ownership transfer itself.
“Investigations must be conducted first, and we need to identify the relevant legal provisions that can be applied,” he said.
“There is a possibility that the allegation is inaccurate, for example, an owner may believe the number belongs to them when in fact it had already been legally transferred previously,” he added.
Elaborating, Aedy said the issue is not solely due to data theft but may involve multiple factors, including the processes and procedures governing ownership transfers.
He said JPJ needs to verify whether the transfer process was carried out legally or otherwise.
“JPJ cannot arbitrarily take action, such as returning the number to the original owner, without a thorough investigation,” he said.
At the same time, he did not rule out the possibility of internal involvement in certain cases, but stressed that careful investigations must be conducted before any conclusions are drawn.
“I do not deny that possibility, but we cannot jump to conclusions so investigations must be conducted on a case-by-case basis,” he added.
It was recently reported that a car rental business owner allegedly found the registration number of his company’s Toyota Alphard was transferred without his consent after it was rented out to a car rental agent in January.
The vehicle bore the popular and unique registration number “P111” and was allegedly taken to a used car showroom in Puchong, Selangor without his knowledge.

Title: JPJ to review viral video of express bus driver allegedly driving dangerously near KM196.1 southbound
Article:
GEORGE TOWN, April 17 — The Road Transport Department (JPJ) will review a recent viral video allegedly showing a bus driver driving dangerously.
JPJ director-general Datuk Aedy Fadly Ramli said they will need to verify the case first.
“Usually, when such issues arise, we will conduct investigations together with police,” he said at a press conference during the launch of a special vehicle registration number series at The Top here today.
He said cases involving criminal elements or accidents will first be referred to the police before any further action is taken.
“Cases involving the police must be referred to them first but as for cases under JPJ’s jurisdiction, particularly those related to the Road Transport Act and not involving criminal elements or accidents, JPJ can take action,” he said.
He said both agencies have a mutual understanding in determining their respective investigative roles to ensure actions taken are in line with legal provisions.
“We have a consensus with the police to determine which cases will be investigated by each party,” he said.
It was reported that police are tracking down an express bus driver believed to have been driving dangerously, as seen in a viral video yesterday.
Alor Gajah district police chief Supt Ahmad Abu Bakar said the footage showed the driver leaning back in his seat and steering with only one hand while the vehicle was moving at night.
He said such behaviour could impair vehicle control, reduce driver focus, and endanger passengers as well as other road users.
He added that the incident is believed to have occurred near Kilometre 196.1 southbound, although investigations are ongoing.
The case is being investigated under Section 42(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987.

Title: JPJ to launch ‘P‑P’ special plate series for 80th anniversary, bidding opens April 20 from RM300
Article:
GEORGE TOWN, April 17 — The Road Transport Department (JPJ) today announced the launch of a special “P-P” vehicle registration series in conjunction with its 80th anniversary.
Bidding for the special “P-P” series is set to open from April 20 to April 24, according to JPJ director-general Datuk Aedy Fadly Ramli.
He said the exclusive series is a symbolic tribute to the department’s 80 years of contributions to Malaysia’s transport system.
“The “P-P” plates will be offered to the public via the JPJ eBid system,” he said in a press conference at The Top here today.
He said bidding for the special plate numbers will only run for five days starting on Monday and will close at 10pm on Friday (April 24).
“Results on the bidding will be announced within 24 hours on April 25,” he said.
Minimum bid prices remain consistent with existing categories, starting from RM300 for running numbers, RM500 for popular numbers, RM2,500 for attractive numbers, RM5,000 for premium numbers, and RM20,000 for golden numbers.
He added that all successful bids must be registered to a vehicle within 12 months from the official results date, in accordance with the terms and conditions of the JPJ eBid system.
“Through enhancements made to JPJ eBid, earlier issues involving system delays and stuck bids have been addressed, and complaints have significantly reduced,” he said.
When asked the reasons behind the “P-P” prefix, he said it reflects Penang where the 80th anniversary celebrations will be held next week.
“It also represented JPJ’s dual roles as penguatkuasaan (enforcement) and pengangkutan (transportation),” he said.
He said nearly 50,000 plate numbers will also be available for direct purchase at 50 per cent discount during the Mega JPJ Madani Carnival at Stadium Batu Kawan next weekend.
“The discounted numbers will only be available for purchase on-site at Stadium Batu Kawan on April 26, with some attractive category numbers priced as low as RM250,” he said.
He added that the full list of available numbers will be released before the carnival.
He said the carnival is from April 24 to 26, so the public is encouraged to attend.

Title: Govt farming scheme earns RM22m, helps over 2,300 Malaysians earn extra income
Article:
PUTRAJAYA, April 17 — The People’s Income Initiative-Agricultural Entrepreneurship (IPR-Intan) programme under the Ministry of Economy has generated cumulative revenue of about RM22 million since its introduction in 2023, said Minister Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir.
He said the result was driven by the participation of more than 2,300 individuals under the programme, including those involved in the cultivation of crops such as chillies and cucumbers.
“One of the (IPR programme) segments is Intan, which focuses on agriculture, and so far it has produced about 2,300 participants with cumulative revenue reaching RM22 million,” he told reporters after visiting the IPR-Intan project site here today.
The IPR-Intan programme not only enables the target group to earn additional income, but also supports the government’s efforts to ensure the country’s basic food supply remains stable and is not disrupted amid the current global energy crisis.
Meanwhile, in Putrajaya, Akmal Nasrullah said the chilli cultivation project using fertigation on 4.04 hectares has benefited 20 participants, each earning an average monthly income of about RM3,000.
“The project, now in its fifth round, has produced over 200 tonnes of chillies worth approximately RM1.5 million, allowing working capital to be sourced from the proceeds.
“The programme demonstrates that assisting the target group is also included in our agenda to raise people’s income, as well as to eradicate poverty,” he said.
Akmal Nasrullah said that this year, the ministry has agreed to implement 20 new IPR-Intan projects, benefiting 600 participants from the B40 group, as well as the poor and hardcore poor.
He said this includes the expansion of the IPR-Intan Putrajaya project, which will see an additional 20 participants, bringing the total to 40.
The IPR-Intan Putrajaya project, which is carried out on land belonging to the Putrajaya Corporation, is managed by the Farmers’ Organisation Authority in collaboration with the Kuala Langat Area Farmers’ Organisation.
The project also received strong support from Nestlé Malaysia, which purchased most of the farm produce for use in its product manufacturing. — Bernama

Title: Malaysia’s inflation remains modest but ticks up in March 2026, govt data shows
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 17 — Malaysia’s inflation increased modestly to 1.7 per cent in March 2026, with the consumer price index (CPI) climbing to 136.4 from 134.1 a year earlier, the Department of Statistics Malaysia (DOSM) said.
In a statement today, chief statistician Datuk Seri Dr Mohd Uzir Mahidin said Malaysia’s inflation in March 2026 was mainly driven by an increase in the transport group to 1.6 per cent from -0.7 per cent in February 2026.
Inflation was also driven by increases in the personal care, social protection and miscellaneous goods and services group, which rose by seven per cent (February 2026: 6.9 per cent). — Bernama

Title: No room for complacency despite upgraded World Bank economic growth forecast, says Mida chairman
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 17 — Malaysia is not only focusing on economic growth but also emphasising building a more resilient and diversified economy in the face of global uncertainties, said Tengku Datuk Seri Zafrul Abdul Aziz.
This follows the World Bank last week raising Malaysia’s growth forecast to 4.4 per cent from 4.1 per cent for 2026, despite increasing global uncertainties, including the West Asia conflict.
Tengku Zafrul, who is the Malaysian Investment Development Authority (Mida) chairman, said the development showed that the country’s economy continued to grow but warned that Malaysia should not get too complacent.
“When global oil prices rise, the impact reaches the cost of goods here. When global trade is tense, our opportunities can also be affected.
“That is why our focus is not just on growth but also on building a more resilient and diversified economy,” he said via his official Instagram.
He added that the World Bank’s upward revision of the forecast was a positive sign, but the future of the country’s economy depended on what was built at the moment. — Bernama

Title: Malaysian tanker with Iraqi crude enters Singapore waters after rare Hormuz transit, heads for Johor
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 17 — The Malaysian crude oil tanker Ocean Thunder entered Singapore waters today as it continued its final approach to Pengerang, Johor.
The
New Straits Times
reported that vessel‑tracking data from the Automatic Identification System showed the Panama‑flagged very large crude carrier moving at 7.1 knots on a 66‑degree course through the Singapore Strait.
The 274‑metre tanker, drawing 17 metres, is carrying about one million barrels of Iraqi Basrah Heavy crude bound for the Pengerang Integrated Petroleum Complex operated by Petronas.
Its position in the strait indicates that berthing and discharge operations are expected to begin once port traffic and clearance allow.
The delivery marks the end of a high‑risk journey that began in Iraq in early March.
The tanker passed through the Strait of Hormuz on April 5, a chokepoint strained by tensions linked to the United States–Israel–Iran conflict.
